deliveryStandards: Treat this PRD as the source of truth for scope and completion. A story is not complete when it only adds typed helpers, mocks, or static examples if the story calls for a runnable app, service, installer, or workflow.
Do not mark a story passed unless the relevant surface can be run manually from this repository with documented commands.

Every story that changes behavior must add automated coverage. Minimum expectation: unit tests for new pure logic, integration tests for service and package boundaries, and end-to-end or smoke coverage for user-facing flows where practical.

Automated tests for product behavior must live with the product code or in a clearly named top-level product test suite. Ralph-only tests are useful but are not sufficient as the sole proof of completion.
When a story changes setup, architecture, commands, or user workflows, the README and any relevant supporting docs must be updated in the same story.

This phase targets a single-user self-hosted system. Auth can remain token-based, but tokens and bootstrap secrets must be configurable through environment variables or config files and must not be hardcoded as the only production path.

This phase may use JSON persistence if it is durable enough for restart recovery, well documented, and covered by tests. If JSON persistence becomes a limiter, SQLite is the preferred next persistence step.
A story that introduces a client application must deliver an actual runnable client package with explicit local run commands, not only a client SDK.
A story that introduces operational behavior must include failure handling, useful errors, and at least one documented manual smoke test path.

userStories.title: Create a pnpm TypeScript monorepo
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want the project organized as a pnpm monorepo with TypeScript-first tooling so that server, runtime, web, mobile, desktop, and shared packages can evolve together.
acceptanceCriteria: The repository uses pnpm workspaces for all first-party apps and packages
All first-party apps and packages build with TypeScript
Shared linting, formatting, and tsconfig rules are centralized
Root commands exist for install, build, lint, typecheck, and test across the full monorepo
The root verification flow is documented and intended to be run locally and in CI
userStories.notes: Implemented a pnpm workspace monorepo with TypeScript app and package scaffolds under apps/ and packages/, centralized root tsconfig/eslint/prettier config, documented root verification in README.md and .github/workflows/verify.yml, and added tests/monorepo.test.ts to verify the workspace contract. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Create shared core packages
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want shared packages for protocol, auth, sessions, ports, providers, and UI primitives so that all clients and services use the same domain model.
acceptanceCriteria: The monorepo includes shared packages for protocol, auth, sessions, ports, providers, and UI
Server, runtime, and clients consume shared types instead of duplicating models
Package boundaries are documented
Changes in shared types are validated by typecheck across all dependent apps
Shared packages expose runtime-safe exports that match their declared package entrypoints
userStories.notes: Replaced the generic shared scaffold with focused workspace packages at packages/protocol, packages/auth, packages/sessions, packages/ports, packages/providers, and packages/ui; updated packages/runtime plus apps/server, apps/web, apps/mobile, and apps/desktop to consume those shared contracts; documented package responsibilities and dependency direction in README.md; and added repo-owned coverage in tests/shared-packages.test.ts plus updated monorepo coverage in tests/monorepo.test.ts. Verified with pnpm verify, including runtime entrypoint checks against built dist exports and typecheck across dependent workspaces.

userStories.title: Run a Remote Dev Console Server control plane
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a central server that manages authentication, hosts, workspaces, sessions, approvals, notifications, and port exposure so that all clients connect through one control plane.
acceptanceCriteria: The server exposes APIs for hosts, workspaces, sessions, approvals, notifications, and forwarded ports
The server supports real-time event delivery to connected clients
The server persists core metadata required to recover state after restart
Unauthorized access to protected resources is rejected
Single-user operator tokens and bootstrap tokens are configurable through environment variables or config files and are not hardcoded as the only usable path
The server has an explicit local run command and documented configuration
The control plane has integration tests that exercise auth, persistence, and real-time event delivery
userStories.notes: Implemented a runnable Node-based control plane in apps/server with protected APIs for hosts, workspaces, sessions, approvals, notifications, forwarded ports, and server-sent events at /api/events; added JSON persistence with restart recovery and serialized writes; made operator/bootstrap tokens configurable through environment variables or a JSON config file; documented local run commands, configuration, and a manual smoke path in README.md; added apps/server package scripts for dev/start; and added integration coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for auth rejection, bootstrap host registration, persistence across restart, and real-time event delivery. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Install the runtime on a remote Linux host
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to install a runtime on a remote Linux machine so that coding agents can run next to the repository and services they need.
acceptanceCriteria: A documented install flow is available for supported Linux systems
The runtime can enroll with the server using a bootstrap token
The runtime reports version, health, and connectivity status
The install flow is safe to rerun on an already configured host
The repo includes a concrete installer or install helper that matches the documented flow
The install and enrollment flow has automated integration coverage
userStories.notes: Implemented a concrete Linux install helper at packages/runtime/scripts/install-linux-runtime.sh plus a runtime CLI for enroll/status reporting in packages/runtime/src/cli.ts and packages/runtime/src/status.ts; documented the supported Linux prerequisites, install steps, rerun-safe behavior, enrollment command, and verification flow in README.md; extended host enrollment records so the runtime reports runtimeVersion, health, connectivity, and lastSeenAt through the control plane; and added product-owned integration coverage in tests/runtime-install.test.ts that builds the runtime, runs the installer twice, enrolls with a bootstrap token, verifies local status output, and confirms the control plane upserts a single host record. Verified with pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck, and pnpm test.

userStories.title: Support local mode with the same runtime contract
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the same runtime model to work locally so that local and remote development follow the same session and workspace lifecycle.
acceptanceCriteria: A local runtime can register with the server or attach directly in development mode
Local workspaces support the same session APIs as remote workspaces
Providers do not require separate local-only implementations
Clients clearly distinguish local and remote hosts
Local and remote flows share the same core runtime abstractions and tests prove the parity
userStories.notes: Extended the shared runtime status contract with hostMode and connectionMode so the same runtime CLI can register a local machine with --host-mode local, while apps/server can also attach a first-class local runtime automatically when REMOTE_AGENT_SERVER_DEVELOPMENT_MODE=true. Updated the control plane to persist and serve attached or registered local/remote host metadata, kept workspace and session APIs unchanged across local and remote hosts, and updated the web and mobile clients to label local versus remote runtimes explicitly. Documented the development-mode and local-registration workflows in README.md plus docs/architecture.md, and added product-owned parity coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for local registration plus attached-local versus remote session lifecycle equivalence, alongside client distinction coverage in tests/web-client-smoke.test.ts and tests/mobile-app-smoke.test.ts. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Register repositories as managed workspaces
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to register local or remote repository paths as workspaces so that sessions always execute against a known codebase.
acceptanceCriteria: A workspace can be created from a repository path on a registered host
Workspace metadata includes host, path, default branch, and runtime association
Invalid or inaccessible paths are rejected with a clear error
Users can list, inspect, and remove registered workspaces
Workspace registration validates that the target path is an accessible git repository
userStories.notes: Implemented repository-backed workspace registration in apps/server so POST /api/workspaces now requires a registered host, validates the target path with git, auto-detects the default branch when omitted, and persists hostId, path, defaultBranch, and runtimeHostId metadata. Added workspace inspect and removal endpoints at GET /api/workspaces/:id and DELETE /api/workspaces/:id, documented the workflow and manual smoke commands in README.md, and added integration coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for successful registration plus list/inspect/remove behavior and clear rejection of unknown hosts, missing paths, and non-git directories. Verified with pnpm lint, pnpm build, pnpm typecheck, and pnpm test.

userStories.title: Start and monitor coding agent sessions
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to start sessions against a workspace and monitor them from web, desktop, or mobile so that I do not need to SSH into the machine to supervise work.
acceptanceCriteria: A session can be started against a chosen workspace and provider
Clients receive live status, logs, and output events
Sessions can be paused, canceled, and resumed
Disconnected clients can reconnect and recover active session state
The core session lifecycle has integration coverage across the server and runtime boundary
userStories.notes: Implemented a managed session lifecycle across packages/runtime and apps/server so POST /api/sessions now validates a registered workspace/provider, starts an in-process runtime session, persists recoverable session state with logs and output history, and exposes GET /api/sessions/:id plus POST /api/sessions/:id/{pause,resume,cancel}. Extended SSE delivery at /api/events to stream runtime-originated session.state.changed, session.log, and session.output events, emit session snapshots for reconnecting clients, and replay missed events with Last-Event-ID. Documented the lifecycle API and manual smoke flow in README.md, and added integration coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for start, live monitoring, pause, reconnect recovery, resume, and cancel across the server/runtime boundary. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Support Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode providers
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a common provider adapter interface so that Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode can all run through the same session model.
acceptanceCriteria: The runtime exposes a provider adapter interface
Claude Code can be launched and monitored through the adapter interface
Codex can be launched and monitored through the adapter interface
OpenCode can be launched and monitored through the adapter interface
Provider-specific failures are surfaced without crashing the session manager
Each provider adapter has contract coverage for launch, output capture, exit handling, and failure reporting
userStories.notes: Implemented a runtime-owned provider adapter contract in packages/runtime/src/provider-adapters.ts and exported it from @remote-agent-server/runtime, with built-in Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode adapters all launched through the shared runtime session manager. Reworked packages/runtime/src/session-manager.ts so provider launch, output, exit, pause/resume, and provider-specific failures flow through that adapter interface and failures transition the session to failed with preserved logs instead of crashing the manager. Documented the adapter-backed session behavior in README.md and added contract coverage in tests/runtime-provider-adapters.test.ts for each provider's launch, output capture, exit handling, and failure reporting, while retaining server/runtime integration coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts. Verified with pnpm lint, pnpm build, pnpm typecheck, and node --import tsx --test tests/server-control-plane.test.ts tests/runtime-provider-adapters.test.ts.

userStories.title: Create isolated worktrees per task
userStories.description: As a developer, I want sessions to optionally run in dedicated git worktrees so that multiple agents can work in parallel without stepping on each other.
acceptanceCriteria: A session can create a dedicated worktree before starting
Worktree metadata is linked to the session
Dirty worktrees are rejected by default unless explicitly allowed
Users can choose direct workspace mode for simple workflows
The implementation includes cleanup behavior or documented cleanup commands for stale worktrees
userStories.notes: Extended session startup in apps/server so POST /api/sessions now supports direct workspace execution by default or isolated git worktree creation with mode=worktree, persists executionPath plus worktree metadata on the session record, and rejects dirty repositories unless allowDirtyWorkspace is explicitly true. Added the shared SessionWorktreeMetadata type in packages/sessions, documented worktree usage and stale-worktree cleanup commands in README.md, and added product-owned integration coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for direct workspace mode, worktree creation, metadata persistence, and dirty-workspace rejection/override. Verified with pnpm lint, pnpm build, pnpm typecheck, and pnpm test:repo.

userStories.title: Review diffs, changed files, and patch summaries
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to review agent output from the client so that I can validate changes without leaving Remote Dev Console.
acceptanceCriteria: Clients can list changed files for a session
Clients can view diffs or patch summaries
Large diffs degrade gracefully through truncation or pagination
The change view clearly identifies added, modified, renamed, and removed files
At least one user-facing client exercises the diff-review flow in an integration or smoke test
userStories.notes: Added git-backed session change review to the control plane with GET /api/sessions/:id/changes for changed-file listings plus patch summaries and GET /api/sessions/:id/diff for paginated diff review with graceful truncation metadata. Extended @remote-agent-server/sessions with shared change-review contracts, added a web client review wrapper in apps/web so a user-facing client can list changes and page through diffs, documented the new review flow in README.md, and added product-owned integration coverage in tests/session-diff-review.test.ts for added, modified, renamed, and removed files plus paginated diff retrieval through both raw server APIs and the web client. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Approve or reject privileged actions
userStories.description: As a developer, I want risky actions to require explicit approval so that agents cannot perform sensitive operations without review.
acceptanceCriteria: Providers can raise approval-required actions through a common interface
Clients can approve or reject pending actions
Approval decisions are stored in an audit log
Rejected actions are surfaced cleanly to the running session
The approval flow is covered by integration tests from request through decision and session outcome
userStories.notes: Extended the shared provider contract with approval request/decision models, taught the runtime session manager and scripted provider adapters to raise privileged actions through that common interface, block sessions while awaiting a decision, resume on approval, and fail cleanly on rejection. Wired the control plane so pending approvals are persisted at /api/approvals, operator clients can approve or reject through POST /api/approvals/:id/decision, approval decisions are appended to a persisted audit log, and rejection outcomes are surfaced back into the running session with explicit log/state updates. Added a web approval client helper in apps/web and product-owned coverage in tests/runtime-provider-adapters.test.ts plus tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for the full request-to-decision-to-session-outcome flow. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Forward remote ports for previews and tools
userStories.description: As a developer, I want built-in remote port forwarding so that I can access preview apps, dashboards, and dev services from any client device.
acceptanceCriteria: Users can manually forward a TCP port from a registered host
Clients can view all active forwarded ports for a workspace or session
Forwarded ports can be opened, closed, and expired
HTTP services are exposed through a managed URL
At least private and shared visibility modes are supported
The port-forwarding lifecycle has integration coverage including expiration behavior
userStories.notes: Extended @remote-agent-server/ports and the control plane so forwarded ports now track open, closed, and expired lifecycle state, validate registered host/workspace/session associations, and expose filtered active listings through GET /api/ports with hostId/workspaceId/sessionId filters plus includeInactive support. Added managed HTTP preview URLs at /ports/:id with shared access or operator-token protection for private forwards, plus POST /api/ports/:id/{open,close,expire} for lifecycle control and automatic expiration on later reads/access when expiresAt has passed. Documented the workflow and manual smoke commands in README.md, and added product-owned integration coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for manual HTTP and TCP forwards, workspace/session listings, managed URL access, private/shared visibility, close/reopen behavior, and expiration handling. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Auto-detect common development ports
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the runtime to detect common listening ports so that previews are easy to find and expose when needed.
acceptanceCriteria: The runtime can detect listening ports associated with active sessions or workspaces
Detected ports appear in clients with suggested labels when possible
Users can promote a detected port into a managed forward
Detected ports are not exposed externally until a user forwards them
Detection and promotion flows are covered by automated integration tests
userStories.notes: Added common development-port detection to @remote-agent-server/runtime using listening-process inspection, plus shared suggested labels in @remote-agent-server/ports. Updated the control plane so GET /api/ports with includeDetected=true returns auto-detected workspace or active-session ports, detected ports remain internal without managed URLs until promoted, and POST /api/ports/:id/open can promote a detected port into a managed forward. Extended the web client to show detected suggestions with an Open forward action, documented the manual detection flow in README.md, and added product-owned coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for detection and promotion plus tests/web-client-smoke.test.ts for the client-facing detected-port promotion flow. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Provide a web client
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a browser-based client so that I can manage sessions, approvals, ports, and previews from any machine.
acceptanceCriteria: The repo includes an actual runnable web app package with explicit local run and build commands
Users can sign in and view hosts, workspaces, sessions, approvals, forwarded ports, and detected ports
The web client can stream live session events
The web client can review diffs and respond to approvals
The web client can open forwarded HTTP previews
The web client has browser-focused integration or end-to-end smoke coverage for the core operator flow
userStories.notes: Implemented a runnable Vite-based browser client in apps/web with documented dev/build/preview commands, persisted operator sign-in, inventory views for hosts/workspaces/sessions/approvals/forwarded ports/detected ports, authenticated live event streaming over /api/events, session diff review, approval decisions, and shared HTTP preview links. Extended the control plane only where needed for the web surface by adding permissive CORS headers for bearer-token browser access and an includeDetected=true read path on GET /api/ports. Added browser-focused product coverage in tests/web-client-smoke.test.ts that renders the real DOM app against a live control plane and exercises sign-in, live events, diff review, approval handling, preview links, and detected-port visibility. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Provide a mobile app with React Native and Expo
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a mobile app built with React Native and Expo so that I can monitor work, approve actions, and open previews from my phone.
acceptanceCriteria: The repo includes an actual runnable Expo app with documented local run commands
The mobile app supports token-based authentication and host or session browsing
The mobile app receives live session state updates
The mobile app can approve or reject pending actions
The mobile app can open forwarded HTTP previews in-app or via the system browser
The mobile app persists its connection settings or token securely enough for a single-user self-hosted setup
The mobile app has smoke or integration coverage for the primary operator flow
userStories.notes: Implemented a runnable Expo mobile app in apps/mobile with documented start, ios, android, and build commands; added a real React Native operator UI for token-based sign-in, host and session browsing, live control-plane event streaming, approval decisions, and forwarded HTTP preview opening in-app or via the system browser; persisted the saved base URL and operator token with Expo SecureStore for this single-user self-hosted setup; updated README.md with local run and manual smoke instructions; and added product-owned smoke coverage in tests/mobile-app-smoke.test.ts for sign-in, live session updates, approval handling, preview opening, and stored-connection bootstrap. Verified with pnpm verify.

userStories.title: Provide a desktop app
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a desktop app so that I can use Remote Dev Console as a primary control surface for both remote and local work.
acceptanceCriteria: The repo includes an actual runnable desktop app package, not only a desktop client library
The desktop app can connect to the server and manage sessions
The desktop app can also work with local runtime flows
The desktop app reuses shared packages from the pnpm monorepo
Users can switch between local and remote workspaces in one interface
The desktop app stores its token or connection secrets in a desktop-appropriate way for a single-user setup
The desktop app has smoke or integration coverage for the primary operator flow
userStories.notes: Implemented a runnable Electron desktop app in apps/desktop with documented start/build commands, a real main/preload/renderer flow, shared monorepo package reuse, and a desktop operator UI that connects to the control plane, starts and manages sessions, surfaces approvals and previews, and switches between local and remote workspaces in one interface. Added desktop connection storage backed by the app data directory with Electron safeStorage when available plus a user-scoped fallback file, and added an on-demand Electron bootstrap script so the app still launches when package-manager install scripts are blocked. Updated README.md with desktop quickstart and smoke guidance, added product-owned smoke coverage in tests/desktop-app-smoke.test.ts for secure connection persistence plus remote and local operator flows, and verified with pnpm verify and pnpm --filter @remote-agent-server/desktop exec electron --version.

userStories.title: Persist session history and resume across devices
userStories.description: As a developer, I want sessions to be resumable from web, desktop, or mobile so that I can move between devices without losing context.
acceptanceCriteria: Session metadata includes provider, workspace, timestamps, and current state
Clients can reopen an existing session from another device
Recovered sessions include recent logs and messages
Temporary disconnects do not destroy active session state
Recovery behavior is covered by automated integration tests across more than one client surface
userStories.notes: Extended the web and mobile operator surfaces so sessions can be explicitly reopened with recovered metadata, recent logs, and recent output messages via Resume context, while preserving the selected session across refresh and reconnect. Added cross-surface recovery coverage in tests/session-recovery-clients.test.ts to reopen the same persisted session from both mobile and web, and retained existing temporary-disconnect recovery evidence in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for SSE reconnect and preserved active session state. Verified with pnpm exec eslint apps/web/src/app.ts apps/mobile/src/controller.ts apps/mobile/src/types.ts apps/mobile/src/MobileOperatorApp.tsx tests/session-recovery-clients.test.ts, pnpm exec tsc -p apps/web/tsconfig.json --noEmit, pnpm exec tsc -p apps/mobile/tsconfig.json --noEmit, pnpm exec tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit, and node --import tsx --test tests/session-recovery-clients.test.ts tests/web-client-smoke.test.ts tests/mobile-app-smoke.test.ts.

userStories.title: Send notifications when user attention is required
userStories.description: As a developer, I want notifications for approvals, failures, and completion so that I can react quickly when I am away from my main machine.
acceptanceCriteria: Users can opt in to notifications on supported clients
The system sends notifications for approval required, failed session, and completed session events
Notifications can deep-link into the related session where supported
Users can mute or customize notification categories
At least one supported client demonstrates end-to-end notification delivery in an automated test or documented smoke flow
userStories.notes: Implemented web-client browser notifications driven by the existing control-plane event stream in apps/web/src/app.ts, with explicit opt-in, per-category muting for approval-required, failed-session, and completed-session alerts, and hash-based session deep links when a browser alert is clicked. Documented the operator flow in README.md, including the manual smoke path for enabling alerts, and added product-owned end-to-end coverage in tests/web-notifications.test.ts while retaining compatibility with tests/web-client-smoke.test.ts. Verified with pnpm exec eslint apps/web/src/app.ts tests/web-notifications.test.ts tests/web-client-smoke.test.ts, pnpm exec tsc -p apps/web/tsconfig.json --noEmit, pnpm exec tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit, pnpm build, and pnpm run test:repo.

userStories.title: Maintain an audit log for sensitive actions
userStories.description: As a maintainer or administrator, I want an audit trail for session and access activity so that sensitive remote actions can be reviewed later.
acceptanceCriteria: The audit log records session lifecycle events, approvals, provider choice, and port exposure changes
Each entry includes actor, target, timestamp, and outcome
Authorized users can inspect the audit log
Critical security-relevant events are not silently dropped
The audit log survives restart and is covered by integration tests
userStories.notes: Extended the control plane audit log in apps/server so sensitive actions now persist audit entries for session lifecycle changes, provider choice on session start, approval requests and decisions, and forwarded-port exposure changes. Added an operator-only inspection endpoint at GET /api/audit-log, made audited state mutations persist with their audit entries in the same write path so security-relevant events are not silently dropped, documented the manual smoke check in README.md, and added product-owned integration coverage in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts for authorized audit-log inspection, actor/target/timestamp/outcome fields, provider/session/approval/port entries, and restart persistence. Verified with pnpm exec tsc -p apps/server/tsconfig.json --noEmit, pnpm exec eslint apps/server/src/index.ts tests/server-control-plane.test.ts, node --import tsx --test tests/server-control-plane.test.ts, and pnpm exec tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit.

userStories.title: Document self-hosting, usage, and development workflows
userStories.description: As a developer, I want clear documentation so that I can self-host the project, connect runtimes, run the clients, and contribute to the pnpm monorepo.
acceptanceCriteria: The root README includes a quickstart for install, local run commands, verification commands, and the main repo layout
Documentation covers server deployment, runtime install, and client setup
Documentation explains the pnpm monorepo layout and developer commands
Documentation includes provider setup for Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode
Documentation explains port forwarding behavior, auth expectations, and security boundaries
Documentation clearly distinguishes what is production-ready, what is MVP, and what is still incomplete

userStories.title: Establish product-owned automated test coverage
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want product-owned unit, integration, and end-to-end tests so that story completion is validated by the application itself rather than only by Ralph.
acceptanceCriteria: Core shared packages have unit tests for their business logic
Server and runtime flows have integration tests that exercise real package boundaries
At least the primary operator journeys for server plus client surfaces have smoke or end-to-end coverage where practical
The root verification command runs product-owned tests in addition to Ralph loop tests
Test ownership and test scope are documented so future stories extend the right test layer
userStories.notes: Extended product-owned unit coverage in tests/shared-packages.test.ts to exercise shared package business logic defaults and factories across auth, protocol, ports, providers, sessions, and ui. Documented test ownership, test scope, and command wiring in README.md so future stories add coverage at the correct layer, and tightened tests/monorepo.test.ts to enforce that documentation plus the root verification contract. Existing product-owned integration and smoke coverage already lived in tests/server-control-plane.test.ts, tests/runtime-install.test.ts, tests/runtime-provider-adapters.test.ts, tests/session-diff-review.test.ts, tests/session-recovery-clients.test.ts, tests/web-client-smoke.test.ts, and tests/mobile-app-smoke.test.ts. Verified with pnpm verify, which runs lint, build, typecheck, product-owned repo tests via test:repo, and Ralph loop tests via test:ralph.

userStories.title: Improve the README for operators and contributors
userStories.description: As an operator or contributor, I want a README that quickly tells me what this project is, what is runnable today, and how to work on it so that I do not need to reverse-engineer the repo.
acceptanceCriteria: The README explains the product at a high level and names the major apps and packages
The README includes a practical quickstart for booting the control plane and any currently runnable clients
The README includes manual smoke-test commands for the MVP
The README explains the branch strategy for Ralph loop attempts and how to start a new attempt
The README links to the deeper docs for runtime install, architecture, provider setup, and security expectations
userStories.notes: Reworked README.md into an operator/contributor entry point that now explains the product at a high level, names the major apps and packages, calls out what is runnable today, includes a practical quickstart for the control plane plus web and mobile clients, and provides copy-paste MVP smoke-test commands. Added a Ralph loop workflow section that explains the branch strategy tied to prd.json.branchName and how to start a new attempt, then linked the README to deeper docs in docs/runtime-install.md, docs/architecture.md, docs/provider-setup.md, and docs/security.md. Added repo-owned coverage in tests/monorepo.test.ts to enforce those README sections and linked docs. Verified with node --import tsx --test tests/monorepo.test.ts tests/shared-packages.test.ts.
